text_original: The purpose of.the magnanimous lies in procuring benefits for the world and eliminating its calamities.
text_normalized: The purpose of.the magnanimous lies in procuring benefits for the world and eliminating its calamities.

text_original: Now among all the current calamities, which are the most important?
text_normalized: Now among all the current calamities, which are the most important?

text_original: The attack on the small states hj the large ones, disturbances of the small houses by the large ones, oppression of the weak by the strong, misuse of the few by the many, deception of the simple by the cunning, disdain towards the humble by the honoured— these are the mis-fortunes in the empire.
text_normalized: The attack on the small states hj the large ones, disturbances of the small houses by the large ones, oppression of the weak by the strong, misuse of the few by the many, deception of the simple by the cunning, disdain towards the humble by the honoured— these are the mis-fortunes in the empire.

text_original: Again, the lack of grace on the part of the ruler, the lack of loyalty on the part of the ruled, the lack of affection on the part of the father, the lack of filial piety on the part of the son — these are further calamities in the empire.
text_normalized: Again, the lack of grace on the part of the ruler, the lack of loyalty on the part of the ruled, the lack of affection on the part of the father, the lack of filial piety on the part of the son — these are further calamities in the empire.

text_original: Also, the mutual injury and harm which the unscrupulous do to one another with weapons, poison, w'ater, and fire is still another calamity in the empire.
text_normalized: Also, the mutual injury and harm which the unscrupulous do to one another with weapons, poison, w'ater, and fire is still another calamity in the empire.

text_original: When we come to think about the cause of all these calamities, how have they arisen?
text_normalized: When we come to think about the cause of all these calamities, how have they arisen?

text_original: Have they arisen out of love of others and benefiting others?
text_normalized: Have they arisen out of love of others and benefiting others?

text_original: Of course we should say no.
text_normalized: Of course we should say no.

text_original: We should say they have arisen out of hate of others and injuring others.
text_normalized: We should say they have arisen out of hate of others and injuring others.

text_original: If we should classify one by one all those who hate others and injure others, should we find them to be universal in love or partial?
text_normalized: If we should classify one by one all those who hate others and injure others, should we find them to be universal in love or partial?

text_original: Of course we should say they are partial.
text_normalized: Of course we should say they are partial.

text_original: Now, since partiality against one another is the cause of the major calamities in the empire, then partiality is wrong.
text_normalized: Now, since partiality against one another is the cause of the major calamities in the empire, then partiality is wrong.

text_original: Motse continued: Whoever critickes others must have something to replace them.
text_normalized: Motse continued: Whoever critickes others must have something to replace them.

text_original: Criticism without suggestion is like trying to stop flood with flood and put out fire with fire.
text_normalized: Criticism without suggestion is like trying to stop flood with flood and put out fire with fire.

text_original: It will surely be without worth.
text_normalized: It will surely be without worth.

text_original: Motse said: Partiality is to be replaced by universality.
text_normalized: Motse said: Partiality is to be replaced by universality.

text_original: But how is it that partiality can be replaced by universality?
text_normalized: But how is it that partiality can be replaced by universality?

text_original: Now, when every one regards the states of others as he regards his own, who would attack the others’ states?
text_normalized: Now, when every one regards the states of others as he regards his own, who would attack the others’ states?

text_original: Others are regarded lile self.
text_normalized: Others are regarded lile self.

text_original: When every one regards the capitals of others as he regards his own, who would seize the others’ capitals?
text_normalized: When every one regards the capitals of others as he regards his own, who would seize the others’ capitals?

text_original: Others are regarded like self.
text_normalized: Others are regarded like self.

text_original: When every one regards the houses of others as he regards his own, who would disturb the others’ houses?
text_normalized: When every one regards the houses of others as he regards his own, who would disturb the others’ houses?

text_original: Others are regarded like self.
text_normalized: Others are regarded like self.

text_original: Now, when the states and cities do not attack and seize each other and when the clans and individuals do not disturb and harm one another — is this a calamity or a benefit to the world?
text_normalized: Now, when the states and cities do not attack and seize each other and when the clans and individuals do not disturb and harm one another — is this a calamity or a benefit to the world?

text_original: Of course it is a Benefit.
text_normalized: Of course it is a Benefit.

text_original: WTien we come to think about the several benefits in regard to their cause, how have they arisen?
text_normalized: WTien we come to think about the several benefits in regard to their cause, how have they arisen?

text_original: Have they arisen out of hate of others and injuring others?
text_normalized: Have they arisen out of hate of others and injuring others?

text_original: Of course we should say no.
text_normalized: Of course we should say no.

text_original: We shouldsay they have arisen out of love of others and benefiting others.
text_normalized: We shouldsay they have arisen out of love of others and benefiting others.

text_original: If we should classify one by one all those who love others and benefit others, should we find them to be partial or universal?
text_normalized: If we should classify one by one all those who love others and benefit others, should we find them to be partial or universal?

text_original: Of course we should say they are universal.
text_normalized: Of course we should say they are universal.

text_original: Now, since universal love is the cause of the major benefits in the world, therefore Motse proclaims universal love is right.
text_normalized: Now, since universal love is the cause of the major benefits in the world, therefore Motse proclaims universal love is right.

text_original: And, as has already been said, the interest of the magnanimous lies in procuring benefits for the world and eliminating its calamities.
text_normalized: And, as has already been said, the interest of the magnanimous lies in procuring benefits for the world and eliminating its calamities.

text_original: Now that we have found out the consequences of universal love to be the major benefits of the world and the consequences of partiality to be the major calamities in the world; this is the reason why Motse said partiality is wrong and universality is right.
text_normalized: Now that we have found out the consequences of universal love to be the major benefits of the world and the consequences of partiality to be the major calamities in the world; this is the reason why Motse said partiality is wrong and universality is right.

text_original: When we try to develop and procure benefits for the world with universal love as one standard, then attentive ears and keen eyes will respond in service to one another, then Ihnhs will be strengthened to work for one another, and those who know the Tao will untiringly instruct others.
text_normalized: When we try to develop and procure benefits for the world with universal love as one standard, then attentive ears and keen eyes will respond in service to one another, then Ihnhs will be strengthened to work for one another, and those who know the Tao will untiringly instruct others.

text_original: Thus the old and those who have neither wife nor children will have the support and supply to spend their old age with, and the young and weak and orphans will have the care and admonition to grow up in.
text_normalized: Thus the old and those who have neither wife nor children will have the support and supply to spend their old age with, and the young and weak and orphans will have the care and admonition to grow up in.

text_original: When universal love is adopted as the standard, then such are the consequent benefits.
text_normalized: When universal love is adopted as the standard, then such are the consequent benefits.

text_original: It is incomprehensible, then, why people should object to universal love when they hear it.
text_normalized: It is incomprehensible, then, why people should object to universal love when they hear it.

text_original: Yet the objection is not all exhausted.
text_normalized: Yet the objection is not all exhausted.

text_original: It is asked: ‘‘ It may be a good thing, but can it be of any use?
text_normalized: It is asked: ‘‘ It may be a good thing, but can it be of any use?

text_original: " Motse replied: If it were not useful then even I would disapprove of it.
text_normalized: " Motse replied: If it were not useful then even I would disapprove of it.

text_original: But how can there be anything that is good but not useful?
text_normalized: But how can there be anything that is good but not useful?

text_original: Let us consider the matter from both sides.
text_normalized: Let us consider the matter from both sides.

text_original: Suppose there are two men.
text_normalized: Suppose there are two men.

text_original: Let one of them hold to partiality and the other to universality.
text_normalized: Let one of them hold to partiality and the other to universality.

text_original: Then the advocate of partiality would say to himself, how can I take care of my friend as I do of myself, how can I take care of his parents as my own?
text_normalized: Then the advocate of partiality would say to himself, how can I take care of my friend as I do of myself, how can I take care of his parents as my own?

text_original: Therefore when he finds his friend hungry he would not feed him, and when he finds him cold he would not clothe him.
text_normalized: Therefore when he finds his friend hungry he would not feed him, and when he finds him cold he would not clothe him.

text_original: In his illness he would not minister to him, and when he is dead he would not bury him.
text_normalized: In his illness he would not minister to him, and when he is dead he would not bury him.

text_original: Such is the word and such is the deed of the advocate of partiality.
text_normalized: Such is the word and such is the deed of the advocate of partiality.

text_original: The advocate of universality is quite unlike this both in word and in deed.
text_normalized: The advocate of universality is quite unlike this both in word and in deed.

text_original: He would say to Mmself, I have lieard that to be a superior man one should take care of his Mend as he does of himself, and take care of his friend's parents as his own.
text_normalized: He would say to Mmself, I have lieard that to be a superior man one should take care of his Mend as he does of himself, and take care of his friend's parents as his own.

text_original: Therefore when he finds his friend hungry he would feed him, and when he finds him cold he would clothe him.
text_normalized: Therefore when he finds his friend hungry he would feed him, and when he finds him cold he would clothe him.

text_original: In his sickness he would serve him, and when he is dead he would bury him.
text_normalized: In his sickness he would serve him, and when he is dead he would bury him.

text_original: Such is the word and such is the deed of the advocate of universality.
text_normalized: Such is the word and such is the deed of the advocate of universality.

text_original: These two persons then are opposed to each other in word and also in deed.
text_normalized: These two persons then are opposed to each other in word and also in deed.

text_original: Suppose they are sincere in word and decisive in deed so that their word and deed are made to agree like the two parts of a tally, and that there is no word but what is realized in deed, then let us consider further: Suppose a war is on, and one is in armour and helmet ready to join the force, life and death are not predictable.
text_normalized: Suppose they are sincere in word and decisive in deed so that their word and deed are made to agree like the two parts of a tally, and that there is no word but what is realized in deed, then let us consider further: Suppose a war is on, and one is in armour and helmet ready to join the force, life and death are not predictable.

text_original: Or suppose one is commissioned a deputy by the ruler to such far countries like Pa, Yiieh, Ch'i, and Ching, and the arrival and return are quite uncertain.
text_normalized: Or suppose one is commissioned a deputy by the ruler to such far countries like Pa, Yiieh, Ch'i, and Ching, and the arrival and return are quite uncertain.

text_original: Now (under such circumstances) let us inquire upon whom would one lay the trust of one’s family and parents.
text_normalized: Now (under such circumstances) let us inquire upon whom would one lay the trust of one’s family and parents.

text_original: Would it be upon the universal Mend or upon the partial friend?
text_normalized: Would it be upon the universal Mend or upon the partial friend?

text_original: It seems to me, on occasions like these, there are no fools in the world.
text_normalized: It seems to me, on occasions like these, there are no fools in the world.

text_original: Even if he is a person who objects to universal love, he will lay the trust upon the universal Mend all the same.
text_normalized: Even if he is a person who objects to universal love, he will lay the trust upon the universal Mend all the same.

text_original: This is verbal objection to the principle but actual selection by it — this is self-contradiction between one’s word and deed.

text_original: It is incomprehensible, then, why people should object to universal love when they hear it.

text_original: Yet the objection is not all exhausted.
text_normalized: Yet the objection is not all exhausted.

text_normalized: It is objected: "91: Maybe it is a good criterion to choose among ordinary men, but it may not apply to the rulers.

text_normalized: Let us again consider the matter from both sides.

text_normalized: Let one of them hold partiality and the other universality.

text_original: Then the partial ruler would say to himself, how can I take care of the people as I do of myself?

text_original: This would be quite contrary to common sense.
text_normalized: This would be quite contrary to common sense.

text_original: But universal love and mutual aid has been personally practised by six ancient sage-kings.

text_original: How do we know they have done it?

text_original: Motse said: I am no contemporary of theirs, neither have I heard their voice or seen their faces.
text_normalized: Motse said: I am no contemporary of theirs, neither have I heard their voice or seen their faces.

text_original: The sources of our knowledge lie in what is written on the bamboos and silk, what is engraved in metal and stones, and what is cut in the vessels to be handed down to posterity.

text_original: The Great Declaration " proclaims: King Wen was like the sun and the moon, shedding glorious and resplendent light in the four quarters as well as over the Western land." universal that it is like the sun and the moon shining upon the world without partiality.

text_original: It happens right in my, Lii’s, time.

text_original: I do not know whether I have wronged Heaven or men.

text_original: Good, I dare not cover up; guilt, I dare not let go — this is clearly seen in the mind of God.

text_original: question, when one does not think in terms of benefits and harm to one’s parents would it be filial piety?

text_original: Motse replied: Now let us inquire about the plans of the filial sons for their parents.
text_normalized: Motse replied: Now let us inquire about the plans of the filial sons for their parents.

text_original: I may ask, when they plan for then parents, whether they desire to have others love or hate them?
text_normalized: I may ask, when they plan for then parents, whether they desire to have others love or hate them?

text_original: Judging from the whole doctrine (of filial piety), it is certain that they desire to have others love their parents.

text_original: Now, what should I do first in order to attain this?
text_normalized: Now, what should I do first in order to attain this?

text_original: Should I first love others’ parents in order that they would love my parents in return, or should I first hate others’ parents in order that they would love my parents in return?

text_original: Of course I should first love others’ parents in order that they would love my parents in return.
text_normalized: Of course I should first love others’ parents in order that they would love my parents in return.

text_original: Hence those who desire to be filial to one another’s parents, if they have to choose (between whether they should love or hate others’ parents), had best first love and benefit others’ parents.
text_normalized: Hence those who desire to be filial to one another’s parents, if they have to choose (between whether they should love or hate others’ parents), had best first love and benefit others’ parents.

text_original: They could not stand np without support, and could not walk without leaning against the wall.
text_normalized: They could not stand np without support, and could not walk without leaning against the wall.

text_original: Now, limited diet is quite hard to endure, and yet it was endured.
text_normalized: Now, limited diet is quite hard to endure, and yet it was endured.

text_original: While Lord Ling encouraged it, his people could be changed within a generation to conform to their superior.
text_normalized: While Lord Ling encouraged it, his people could be changed within a generation to conform to their superior.

text_original: Lord Kou Chien of the state of Yiieh admired courage and taught it his ministers and soldiers three years.
text_normalized: Lord Kou Chien of the state of Yiieh admired courage and taught it his ministers and soldiers three years.

text_original: Fearing that their knowledge had not yet made them efficient he let a fire be set on the boat, and beat the drum to signal advance.
text_normalized: Fearing that their knowledge had not yet made them efficient he let a fire be set on the boat, and beat the drum to signal advance.

text_original: The soldiers at the bead of the ranli: were even pushed down.
text_normalized: The soldiers at the bead of the ranli: were even pushed down.

text_original: Those who perished in the flames and in water were numberless.
text_normalized: Those who perished in the flames and in water were numberless.

text_original: Even then they would not retreat without signal.
text_normalized: Even then they would not retreat without signal.

text_original: The soldiers of Yiieh would be quite terri&ed (ordinarily).
text_normalized: The soldiers of Yiieh would be quite terri&ed (ordinarily).

text_original: To be burnt alive is a hard task, and yet it was accomplished.
text_normalized: To be burnt alive is a hard task, and yet it was accomplished.

text_original: When the Lord of Yiieh encouraged it, his people could be changed within a generation to conform to their superior.
text_normalized: When the Lord of Yiieh encouraged it, his people could be changed within a generation to conform to their superior.

text_original: Lord Wen of the state of Chin liked coarse clothing.
text_normalized: Lord Wen of the state of Chin liked coarse clothing.

text_original: And so in his time the people of Chin wore suits of plain cloth, jackets of sheep skin, hats of spun silk, and big rough shoes.
text_normalized: And so in his time the people of Chin wore suits of plain cloth, jackets of sheep skin, hats of spun silk, and big rough shoes.

text_original: Thus attired, they would go in and see the Lord and come out and walk in the court.
text_normalized: Thus attired, they would go in and see the Lord and come out and walk in the court.

text_original: To dress up in coarse clothing is hard to do, yet it has been done.
text_normalized: To dress up in coarse clothing is hard to do, yet it has been done.

text_original: When Lord Wen encouraged it his people could be changed within a generation to conform to their superior.
text_normalized: When Lord Wen encouraged it his people could be changed within a generation to conform to their superior.

text_original: Now to endure limited diet, to be burnt alive, and to wear coarse clothing are the hardest things in the world, yet when the superiors encouraged them the people could be changed within a generation.
text_normalized: Now to endure limited diet, to be burnt alive, and to wear coarse clothing are the hardest things in the world, yet when the superiors encouraged them the people could be changed within a generation.

text_original: Why was this so?
text_normalized: Why was this so?

text_original: It was due to the desire to conform to the superior.
text_normalized: It was due to the desire to conform to the superior.

text_original: Now, as to universal love and mutual aid, they are beneficial and easy beyond a doubt.
text_normalized: Now, as to universal love and mutual aid, they are beneficial and easy beyond a doubt.

text_original: It seems to me that the only trouble is that there is no superior who encourages it.
text_normalized: It seems to me that the only trouble is that there is no superior who encourages it.

text_original: If there is a superior who encourages it, promoting it with rewards and commendations, threatening its reverse with punishments, I feel people will tend toward universal love and mutual aid like fire tending upward and water downwards — it will be unpreventable in the world.
text_normalized: If there is a superior who encourages it, promoting it with rewards and commendations, threatening its reverse with punishments, I feel people will tend toward universal love and mutual aid like fire tending upward and water downwards — it will be unpreventable in the world.

text_original: Therefore, universal love is really the way of the sage-kings.
text_normalized: Therefore, universal love is really the way of the sage-kings.

text_original: It is what gives peace to the rulers and sustenance to the people.
text_normalized: It is what gives peace to the rulers and sustenance to the people.

text_original: The gentleman would do well to understand and practise universal love; then he would be gracious as a ruler, loyal as a minister, affectionate as a father, filial as a son, courteous as an elder brother, and respectful as a younger brother.
text_normalized: The gentleman would do well to understand and practise universal love; then he would be gracious as a ruler, loyal as a minister, affectionate as a father, filial as a son, courteous as an elder brother, and respectful as a younger brother.

text_original: So, if the gentleman desires to be a gracious ruler, a loyal minister, an affectionate father, a filial son, a courteous elder brother, and a respectful younger brother, universal love must be practised.
text_normalized: So, if the gentleman desires to be a gracious ruler, a loyal minister, an affectionate father, a filial son, a courteous elder brother, and a respectful younger brother, universal love must be practised.

text_original: It is the way of the sage-kings and the great blessing of the people.
text_normalized: It is the way of the sage-kings and the great blessing of the people.
